finding: The aorta is normally very resistant to infection. However, an abnormal aortic wall as occurs with

differentials.name: Bacterial: Staphylococcus > Streptococcus > Salmonella, Clostridium septicum
differentials.raw: Bacterial: Staphylococcus > Streptococcus > Salmonella, Clostridium septicum (exceedingly rare, but strongly associated with GI / hematologic malignancy)

differentials.name: Stenosis of coronary ostia ← intimal thickening
differentials.raw: Stenosis of coronary ostia ← intimal thickening

differentials.name: Aortic regurgitation
differentials.raw: Aortic regurgitation (syphilitic valvulitis), rare DDx:  Degenerative calcification of ascending aorta (older population, no aneurysm, no aortic regurgitation)

context: atherosclerotic disease, preexisting aneurysm, cystic medial necrosis, diabetes, vascular malformation, medical devices, or surgery, makes it susceptible to infection! Organism: √  thickening of aortic wall + intramural gas (KEY FEATURE) N.B.:   aorta may appear normal on imaging Rx:   empiric antibiotic therapy against S. aureus and gram-negative rods DDx:   infected aortic aneurysm (dilated aorta prone to rupture) Syphilitic Aortitis =  LUETIC AORTITIS Prevalence:   in 10-15% of untreated patients (accounts for death in ⅓) Path:  periaortitis (via lymphatics), mesaortitis (via vasa vasorum)
